text: This video will demonstrate a laparoscopic appendectomy for acute appendicitis. The appendix is an organ at the base of the colon called the cecum with no discrete function. It is fed by a single artery called the appendiceal artery. Occasionally, it can become obstructed, leading to acute appendicitis.
The surgeon places trocars or ports into the patient's abdominal cavity to establish access for the laparoscopic appendectomy procedure.

The laparoscopic view reveals small incidental hernias in the abdominal wall. In the left portion of the screen, the inflamed and dilated appendix is visible among the small bowel, with dilated blood vessels on its surface and a white exudate or inflammatory rind surrounding it.

The video shows a normal appendix, which appears soft and pliable without any signs of inflammation. The appendix lacks an inflammatory rind, is not dilated, and the blood vessels on its surface are not engorged.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to gently separate the white inflammatory rind from the inflamed appendix and adjacent structures, while the appendix is grasped for stabilization during the dissection.

In the laparoscopic view, the surgeon encounters bleeding from the dilated and engorged blood vessels surrounding the inflamed appendix. The bleeding is localized using a suction irrigator device and controlled with an electrosurgical instrument.

The surgeon uses an electrosurgical instrument to divide attachments, exposing the base of the appendix. Suction is applied along the mesentery and fatty tissue surrounding the appendix, revealing the appendiceal artery.
The surgeon encounters difficulty localizing the appendiceal artery due to inflammation. Clips are applied to control any bleeding, resulting in minimal blood loss.

The surgeon divides the artery using cautery and proceeds to sharply dissect the remainder of the appendix. After controlling the artery, the dissection progresses rapidly towards the base of the appendix.

The surgeon uses both blunt and sharp dissection techniques, employing electrocautery to control bleeding while gently pushing tissues away from the appendix. The cecum is exposed to ensure complete removal of the appendix, preventing any residual tissue that could lead to recurrent appendicitis.
The surgeon skeletonizes the appendix at its base, meticulously removing surrounding tissue to ensure a secure staple line for division.

The surgeon divides the final mesenteric attachments to the appendix, then places a stapling device at the base of the appendix, removing a small portion of the cecum to ensure complete removal of the appendix.
This avoids the complication of a partial appendectomy and a potential diagnostic dilemma in the future if appendicitis were to recur.

The surgeon places the removed appendix into a specimen bag to isolate it from the rest of the abdominal cavity. The staple line is inspected for integrity, and the area is irrigated to check for bleeding and clear any residual infectious fluids.

The surgeon irrigates the pelvis, lifting the uterus to access deeper areas where pus may accumulate due to gravity. The irrigation continues until the fluid returns clear, indicating the removal of potential infectious material.
The surgeon removes the appendix from the abdominal cavity using an endocatch bag, minimizing the risk of wound infection by isolating the appendix from surrounding tissues.
